probe_designs.content: Primary fairness probe: compute the equal-opportunity recall/TPR gap for the DEATH class across the 5 ethnicity groups (white, black, hispanic, asian, other) at the HARD-FROZEN threshold 0.5 (constant inside prober.py). Measure = max_g(TPR_g) - min_g(TPR_g). Healthy signal: gap < 0.05 (parity within sampling noise). Unhealthy signal: gap >= 0.10, indicating the model systematically under-detects deaths for at least one ethnicity. Must NOT use AUROC. Must NOT allow threshold tuning or per-group offset shifting to mask the disparity.
possible_sources: Hardt et al., 'Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning' (NeurIPS 2016)
Fairlearn: Assessment module for equalized odds and equal opportunity
MIMIC-III benchmarking papers on fairness in ICU outcome prediction

probe_designs.content: Even though AUROC is misleadingly optimistic for the overall imbalanced task, per-ethnicity AUROC still reveals whether the model discriminates equally well across groups before thresholding. Compute per-group AUROC for positive (death) class. Healthy: std across groups < 0.03 and min group AUROC within 0.05 of mean. Unhealthy: std >= 0.05 or any group AUROC drops below 0.70, indicating representation/feature learning harms specific populations before any decision threshold.
possible_sources: Harutyunyan et al., 'Multitask Learning and Benchmarking with Clinical Time Series Data' (MIMIC benchmarks)
Chen et al., 'The Gap of AI Predictive Medicine on Ethnicity' (JAMIA)
scikit-learn metrics: roc_auc_score with per-group masking

probe_designs.content: Inspect calibration per-ethnicity via reliability diagrams and Brier score per group. For each ethnicity, bin predicted probabilities (e.g., quintiles) and compare predicted mean probability vs observed prevalence in the death class. Healthy: per-group ECE (Expected Calibration Error) < 0.05 and Brier scores within 20% of each other. Unhealthy: severe miscalibration (>0.10 ECE) or systematic over-prediction for one group and under-prediction for another, even if recall gap looks OK at threshold 0.5.
possible_sources: Guo et al., 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks' (ICML 2017)
Niculescu-Mizil & Caruana, 'Predicting Good Probabilities with Supervised Learning' (ICML 2005)
Platt scaling and isotonic regression literature
reliability-diagram tooling in sklearn/calibration.py or MLCalibration package

probe_designs.content: Equal-opportunity gap alone can be gamed by collapsing all groups to near-zero recall. Complement the gap probe by enforcing an ABSOLUTE recall floor: every ethnicity group must achieve recall at threshold 0.5 >= some minimum (e.g., >= 0.30, or within 2x of best group). Healthy: all 5 groups have meaningful recall levels AND small gap. Unhealthy: small gap but near-zero recall across all groups, OR a healthy global recall achieved by collapsing minority groups to trivial recall (< 0.10).
possible_sources: Dwork et al., 'Fairness Through Awareness'
Selbst et al., 'Fairness and Abstraction in Sociotechnical Systems' (FAT* 2019)
Fairness literature on 'level-down' gaming of equal-opportunity constraints

probe_designs.content: The ANCHOR guards train.py's own utility: weighted-BCE loss on train + AUPRC on validation. The fairness fix must not sacrifice these. Record the baseline AUPRC (from unmodified train.py) and verify post-fix AUPRC degrades by <= 20%. Healthy: AUPRC within 5% of baseline or improved. Unhealthy: AUPRC drops > 20% (auto-revert triggered by anchor guard), indicating the 'fairness fix' achieved parity by destroying the model's utility.
possible_sources: Anchor guard design in AutoProbe codebase (see anchor-guard-utility-floor memory)
Davis & Goadrich, 'The Relationship Between Precision-Recall and ROC Curves' (ICML 2006)
Saito & Rehmsmeier, 'The Precision-Recall Plot Is More Informative than the ROC Plot' (PLOS ONE 2015)

probe_designs.content: Asian and Other ethnicity groups in MIMIC-III are typically very small (often < 5% of total cohort). Bootstrap the per-group recall at threshold 0.5 across 200 resamples of the validation set and compute 95% CI for each group. Healthy: CI width for every group < 0.05, meaning the gap measurement is stable. Unhealthy: CI width for Asian/Other >= 0.10, meaning reported gap is dominated by sampling noise and cannot reliably distinguish genuine fairness from statistical fluctuation — the prober may pass spuriously or fail spuriously.
possible_sources: Efron, 'The Jackknife, the Bootstrap and other Resampling Plans' (SIAM 1982)
MIMIC-III cohort description papers (Johnson et al., Sci Data 2016) — demographic breakdowns
Bootstrap CI methodology for small-subgroup evaluation in clinical ML

probe_designs.content: For the TRUE-deaths subset only, compare the distribution of predicted P(death) across the 5 ethnicities using statistical tests (Kolmogorov-SmirNV between all pairs, or Kruskal-Wallis + follow-up). Healthy: distributions statistically indistinguishable (p > 0.05 with multiple-testing correction) → model scores true deaths similarly across groups. Unhealthy: systematic shift, e.g., Black/Hispanic true deaths scored lower than White true deaths at the 0.5 threshold, revealing the source of recall disparity even before hard thresholding.
possible_sources: Larson et al., 'The Accuracy, Fairness, and Limits of Predicting Recidivism' (FAT* 2018) — score distribution analysis
Buolamwini & Gebru, 'Gender Shades' project — per-group score auditing
scipy.stats ks_2samp / kruskal for distribution-shift testing

probe_designs.content: In the death class (positives), analyze WHICH patients are FN'd (missed at threshold 0.5) vs TP'd by ethnicity. Compute the FN rate per ethnicity among the ground-truth deaths. Cross-tab with confounders (age, gender, admission type, severity scores like SOFA/APACHE II if available) to see if an ethnicity's excess FNs are explained by genuine confounders (acceptable) or by raw ethnicity (unacceptable — the model's errors are ethnicity-discriminatory conditional on clinical severity). Healthy: FN rate differences explained by severity distribution differences. Unhealthy: FN disparity persists after controlling for severity.
possible_sources: Obermeyer et al., 'Dissecting Racial Bias in an Algorithm Used to Manage the Health of Populations' (Science 2019)
Vyas et al., 'Hidden in Plain Sight: Race and Algorithmic Bias in Health Care' (NEJM 2020)
MIMIC-III severity scores: SOFA/APACHE II for confounder adjustment

probe_designs.content: Even though threshold 0.5 is frozen for the GAP measurement, inspect the LOCAL smoothness of each group's TPR curve around 0.5 (e.g., sweep 0.40 → 0.60 in steps of 0.02). Healthy: per-group TPR curves are monotone-smooth and groups' curves are close in shape. Unhealthy: sharp jumps, crossings, or erratic behavior near 0.5 for some groups → the 'parity' at exactly 0.5 is a fragile, threshold-cherry-picked artifact that breaks under tiny threshold shifts (suggesting score clipping, degenerate features, or overfit to the specific 0.5 point).
possible_sources: Threshold sensitivity analysis in clinical decision-curve analysis (Vickers & Elkin, Medical Decision Making 2006)
scikit-learn precision_recall_curve for threshold sweep
Robustness auditing practices in ML fairness literature

probe_designs.content: Treat the pre-final-layer embeddings of train.py as learned features. Train a LINEAR probe to predict ethnicity one-hot (5-class softmax) from these embeddings on a held-out split. If the probe achieves high accuracy (> ~0.50-0.60, far above the ~0.20 base rate), the representations encode ethnicity as a strong signal — a potential shortcut that allows the model to satisfy the fairness probe via superficial re-weighting rather than genuinely reducing recall disparity. Healthy: linear probe accuracy close to base rate. Unhealthy: linear probe accuracy far exceeds base rate, revealing ethnicity-encoded representations that the model could exploit for fairness-gaming.
possible_sources: Alain & Bengio, 'Understanding Intermediate Layers Using Linear Classifier Probes' (ICLR 2017)
Representation-learning auditing in fairness literature
Adebayo et al., 'Sanity Checks for Saliency Maps' (NeurIPS 2018) — similar audit philosophy
